page: Amazon Web Services c6in.32xlarge is an x86_64 compute-optimized server powered by the Intel Xeon 8375C processor. Running on the Nitro hypervisor, it features 128 vCPUs across 64 physical cores with hyper-threading enabled. The system operates at a clock speed of 3.5 GHz and includes 256.0 GB of DDR4 memory running at 3 GHz, resulting in a memory-to-core ratio of 2.0 GB per core. It does not include local storage or GPUs, relying instead on network-attached storage and a baseline network bandwidth of 200 Gbps.

Benchmark data shows top-tier multi-core CPU performance, with the stress-ng multi-core, passmark CPU, and passmark CPU extended instructions tests all scoring in the top 10%. Single-core performance is moderate, with stress-ng single-core and geekbench single-core scores falling in the middle 50%. Memory bandwidth is strong, placing in the top 25% for both read and write operations. Cryptographic capabilities are also in the top 10%, supported by hardware-accelerated instruction sets like AES, VAES, and SHA_NI.

The server offers high compute density and network throughput, making it suitable for network-bound compute workloads, static web serving, and database operations. However, the lack of local storage requires reliance on external EBS volumes, which may introduce latency tradeoffs compared to local NVMe options. The 2.0 GB of RAM per core is optimized for compute-heavy tasks rather than memory-intensive databases. Appropriate workloads include multi-threaded batch processing, static web serving, and moderate LLM prompt processing.
description: Amazon Web Services c6in.32xlarge is an x86_64 compute-optimized server utilizing the Nitro hypervisor. It is equipped with an Intel Xeon 8375C processor providing 128 vCPUs across 64 physical cores at 3.5 GHz, alongside 256.0 GB of DDR4 memory. The instance features a high baseline network bandwidth of 200 Gbps but lacks local storage and dedicated GPUs. In benchmark evaluations, the server demonstrates top-tier multi-core performance, ranking in the top 10% for stress-ng multi-core and passmark CPU tests, while single-core performance remains in the middle 50%. Its memory bandwidth and database operations rank in the top 25%. This hardware profile is suited for network-intensive compute workloads, static web serving, and multi-threaded applications, though the lack of local storage requires external EBS volumes for data persistence.
